mainmatter.p: If a sequence \cdots \xrightarrow { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B } \xrightarrow{g} { C } \xrightarrow \cdots in an abelian category is exact at B, then it is complex.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: By exactness at B we may assume \operatorname{ker} {g}= \operatorname{im} {f}; we must show that f;g=0. Based on our assumption, we see that f;g factors through the zero morphism as follows:
Any morphism factoring through zero must be zero.

mainmatter.p: Some notes on abelian categories, based on my study of Vakil’s The Rising Sea.

Let \cdots \xrightarrow { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B } \xrightarrow{g} { C } \xrightarrow \cdots be a sequence in an abelian category \mathcal{A}. The term B is called complex when f;g=0, and exact when the kernel of g is isomorphic to the image of f, in other words \operatorname{ker} {g}= \operatorname{im} {f}.
An exact sequence in an abelian category is a sequence of maps whose interior terms are all exact. A complex sequence is one whose interior terms are all complex.

A short exact sequence in an abelian category \mathcal{A} is an exact sequence of the form \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{A} } \xrightarrow{ } A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C \xrightarrow{ } \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{A} }.
If a sequence \cdots \xrightarrow { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B } \xrightarrow{g} { C } \xrightarrow \cdots in an abelian category is exact at B, then it is complex.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: By exactness at B we may assume \operatorname{ker} {g}= \operatorname{im} {f}; we must show that f;g=0. Based on our assumption, we see that f;g factors through the zero morphism as follows:
Any morphism factoring through zero must be zero.

mainmatter.p: A sequence \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{A} } \xrightarrow A \xrightarrow{f} Bin an abelian category \mathcal{A} is exact if and only if A \xrightarrow{f} B is a monomorphism.

block.headline: Proof (\Rightarrow).
block.p: Suppose that \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{A} } \xrightarrow A \xrightarrow{f} B is exact, and fix X \xrightarrow{g,h} A such that g;f = h;f to check that g=h. Considering that \mathcal{A} is abelian, this is the same as to check that (g-h);f = 0 implies g-h=0. Using our assumption, we may factor g-h uniquely through the kernel of f as follows:
As \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{A} } \xrightarrow A \xrightarrow{f} B is exact, the kernel of f is in fact the zero object. Therefore g-h=0 and thus g=h.

block.headline: Proof (\Leftarrow).
block.p: On the other hand, suppose that A \xrightarrow{f} B is a monomorphism; we must check that \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{A} } \xrightarrow A \xrightarrow{f} B is exact, i.e. \operatorname{ker} {f} = \operatorname{im} { { \left ( \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{A} } \xrightarrow A \right )} } = \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{A} }. We will check that the following diagram satisfies the universal property of the kernel:
We fix X \xrightarrow{h} A factoring the zero morphism through f to check the universal property:

Observe that the universal map depicted makes the diagram commute if and only if h=0. To see that this is the case, we will use the fact that f is a monomorphism: as X \xrightarrow{h} A \xrightarrow{f} B and X \xrightarrow{0} A \xrightarrow{f} B are equal, it follows that h=0.

mainmatter.p: Let \cdots \xrightarrow { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B } \xrightarrow{g} { C } \xrightarrow \cdots be a sequence in an abelian category \mathcal{A}. The term B is called complex when f;g=0, and exact when the kernel of g is isomorphic to the image of f, in other words \operatorname{ker} {g}= \operatorname{im} {f}.
